senses.definition: used to suggest that negative information or gossip tends to spread quickly, emphasizing the idea that people are often more interested in hearing and sharing bad news than good news

examples.x: In the world of social media, bad news travels fast, and a single post can ruin a person's reputation.

senses.definition: used to imply that negative or distressing information tends to spread quickly, and that people are often more eager to share or spread negative news than positive news
used to suggest that in the absence of any information, one can assume that everything is going well or according to plan

examples.x: The doctor said he would call if the test results were abnormal, but no news is good news, and the patient assumed everything was fine.

word_list.word: there is no smoke without fire
word_list.pronunciation: ðɛɹ ɪz nˈoʊ smˈoʊk wɪðˌaʊt fˈaɪɚ

senses.definition: used to suggest that even if the details of a particular story or rumor are not entirely accurate, there is usually some underlying truth or basis for the rumor

word_list.word: gossiping and lying go hand in hand
word_list.pronunciation: ɡˈɑːsɪpɪŋ ænd lˈaɪɪŋ ɡˌoʊ hˈænd ɪn hˈænd

senses.definition: used to imply that people who engage in gossip are often prone to lying or spreading false information
used to warn against the dangers of careless talk or gossip, particularly in situations where secrecy or confidentiality is important, as it can have serious consequences

examples.x: She knew that loose lips sink ships, so she kept the details of her new project to herself until it was ready to be launched.

word_list.word: a tale never loses in the telling
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ tˈeɪl nˈɛvɚ lˈuːzᵻz ɪnðə tˈɛlɪŋ

senses.definition: used to suggest that stories or rumors tend to become more exaggerated or distorted as they are passed on, and so one should take steps to verify information before accepting it as true
